lines.text: Chapter five, section one. The parts-devices-systems hierarchy, made concrete.
Chapters two and three introduced the parts-devices-systems framework as a conceptual structure for engineering biology.
Chapter five takes that framework and populates it with real biological components.
After this chapter you will know the actual menu of parts, what kinds of devices are built from them, and what simple systems have been demonstrated.
This is the chapter where the abstraction stops being abstract.
The hierarchy reminded briefly.
A part is a piece of DNA that encodes one biological function — a promoter, an operator, a ribosome binding site, a protein-coding sequence, a terminator.
A device is built from a collection of parts and performs a higher-order user-defined function — a protein generator, an inverter, a sender of a chemical signal, a logic gate.
A system is built from one or more devices, and performs some task at the level of the cell or population — an oscillator, a counter, an edge-detection algorithm, eventually a metabolic pathway.

Parts and devices are usually treated as modular — assumed to behave the same way regardless of what they are combined with — but the textbook is honest from the start that this is a working assumption rather than a clean fact of biology.
Context dependence is real, and we will spend the last sections of this chapter on the engineering work being done to mitigate it.
The other concept worth pinning down: chassis.
The chassis is the underlying biology in which your system runs.
It provides the transcription and translation machinery, the metabolic substrates, the membranes and other cellular infrastructure that an engineered circuit relies on.
Most often the chassis is a living cell — E. coli, S. cerevisiae yeast, occasionally a mammalian cell line — running the synthetic biology system in vivo.
But the chassis can also be abiotic — a tube containing just the necessary purified components for transcription and translation, running the system in vitro.
Cell-free synthetic biology, where engineered systems run in tubes rather than cells, is a growing subfield, and we will see it again.
There is one piece of standard notation to flag.
The Synthetic Biology Open Language, abbreviated SBOL, defines a visual notation — a set of standard symbols — for drawing biological parts and devices on a page.
Promoter, operator, RBS, coding sequence, terminator all have standardised pictograms, and chained together they form schematic diagrams that look a lot like circuit diagrams in electronics.
SBOL Visual is increasingly used in the literature, and you will see those symbols throughout this chapter.
